bullets.summary: NVIDIA Q3 FY22 (ended Oct 31, 2021) revenue hit a record $7.10B, up 50% YoY and 9% QoQ, with Gaming and Data Center both posting all-time highs; EPS of $1.17 non-GAAP, up 60% YoY.

GPU shortage persisted throughout 2021; NVIDIA's CFO said demand would continue to exceed supply for most of the year, with gaming cards scarce and crypto miners absorbing a material share of Ampere supply.
NVDA full-year 2021 total return was approximately +125%, reflecting compounding demand across gaming and data center as the stock re-rated substantially during the year.
Apple Q4 FY21 (ended Sep 25, 2021) revenue was $83.4B, up 29% YoY; Services set an all-time record at $18.3B, up 26%, with 70.5% gross margin on Services versus 34.3% on Products.
AAPL stock rose 34% in 2021; by December 2021, analysts were debating whether Apple would become the first US company to reach a $3 trillion market cap, with the stock trading near $178-$182.

Supply constraints cut an estimated $6B from Apple's Q4 FY21 revenue due to industry-wide chip shortages and COVID-related manufacturing disruptions, yet the stock absorbed the miss and continued to rally.
Microsoft Q1 FY22 (ended Sep 30, 2021) Intelligent Cloud revenue was $16.96B, up 31% YoY, beating estimates of $16.51B; Azure and other cloud services grew 50% YoY.
Microsoft FY21 (ended Jun 30, 2021) total revenue was $168B, up 18% YoY; operating income grew 32% to $70B; LinkedIn and security each exceeded $10B in annual revenue for the first time.
MSFT stock delivered a strong return in 2021, driven by Azure cloud growth and Teams adoption momentum from the pandemic-era shift to remote work.
Alphabet Q3 2021 consolidated revenue was $65.1B, up 41% YoY; Google Search was $37.9B; YouTube ads $7.2B (+43%); Google Cloud $5.0B (+45%) though still operating at a -$644M loss.
GOOGL stock surged approximately 65% in 2021, outperforming every other major Big Tech name and more than tripling the S&P 500's gain.

BOTZ (Global X Robotics & Artificial Intelligence ETF) returned approximately +8.65% in 2021, significantly underperforming the S&P 500 (+28.75%) despite holding robotics and automation names such as NVIDIA, Keyence, Fanuc, and Intuitive Surgical.

BOTZ's top holdings included Japanese industrial automation names (Keyence, Fanuc, Mitsubishi Electric) representing more than half the fund's non-US exposure, weighting the ETF toward slower-moving industrial cycles rather than hyper-growth tech.

IBM unveiled the Eagle processor on November 16, 2021 — a 127-qubit chip described as the first whose quantum state cannot be reliably simulated on a classical computer — marking the first time IBM broke the 100-qubit barrier.

QTUM (Defiance Quantum ETF) returned +35.18% in 2021, well above its category average of +15.09%, as machine learning and quantum-adjacent semiconductor names (Intel, Micron, STMicro) contributed; quantum computing itself remains pre-commercial.
SPY total return in 2021 was approximately +28.75%; the S&P 500 closed at ~4,766 on December 31, 2021, a full-year gain of about 27%.

As of December 2021, Wall Street 2022 outlooks ranged from bearish (Morgan Stanley: -3.7%) to bullish (Goldman Sachs: +11.7%), with consensus concern centered on the Fed tapering its asset purchases faster than expected due to persistent inflation.
AGG (iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF) returned approximately -1.8% in 2021 — its yield of roughly 1.81% was far below CPI, which hit 6.2% in October 2021, making real returns deeply negative.

Rising rate expectations driven by the Fed signaling faster taper of asset purchases pushed bond prices lower across the year; AGG's 2021 loss was the first of what would be two consecutive down years for the ETF.

BIL (SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF) tracks US Treasury bills with 1-3 month maturities; with the Fed funds rate at 0-0.25% throughout 2021, BIL's yield was effectively near zero, delivering minimal positive return for the year.

Gold ended 2021 lower than it began, posting a negative return for the year despite CPI rising to multi-decade highs; after a strong 2020 driven by pandemic safe-haven demand, gold gave back ground as real yields rose on Fed taper expectations.

VIG (Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF) returned +23.76% in 2021, trailing the S&P 500 by approximately 5pp; the ETF holds dividend-growth companies with long track records of consecutive annual dividend increases.

NVDA.rationale: Q3 FY22 revenue of $7.10B was up 50% YoY with both Gaming and Data Center at record highs (Nov 17, 2021 earnings), and the full-year 2021 stock return of ~125% reflects compounding adoption across AI-adjacent compute workloads and gaming demand, with supply still constrained — real revenue acceleration with apparent room to run.

AAPL.rationale: By December 2021, AAPL was being discussed as imminently hitting a $3 trillion market cap (Motley Fool, Dec 15, 2021), stock had already risen 34% for the year, and sell-side enthusiasm was the dominant narrative even as supply constraints shaved $6B from Q4 FY21 revenue — valuation stretched and enthusiasm is the story.

MSFT.rationale: Azure grew 50% YoY in Q1 FY22 (reported Oct 26, 2021), Intelligent Cloud revenue hit $16.96B beating estimates, and FY21 operating income grew 32% — cloud adoption is compounding with genuine revenue acceleration and Azure is still expanding market share.

GOOGL.rationale: Q3 2021 revenue of $65.1B grew 41% YoY with Google Cloud at +45% (Oct 26, 2021), and the full-year stock gain of ~65% reflects genuine advertising-revenue compounding rather than pure sentiment — the cloud segment was still at operating loss, indicating meaningful investment phase with adoption still expanding.

BOTZ.rationale: BOTZ returned only +8.65% in 2021 while the broad market was up ~28.75%, but the available bullets do not contain enough specific dated news to classify this as buildup versus digestion — the underperformance could reflect the Japan-heavy, industrial-cycle weighting rather than a clear wave-stage signal for the robotics theme.

QTUM.rationale: IBM's Eagle 127-qubit processor (Nov 16, 2021) is a genuine research milestone — the first chip to surpass 100 qubits and exceed classical simulation capacity — yet quantum computing remains entirely pre-commercial revenue; the +35.18% QTUM return is driven by machine learning semiconductor proxies rather than quantum revenue, leaving the wave quiet and under-owned.

SPY.rationale: SPY is a general-markets ticker; no specific wave bucket applies, and the 2021 return of +28.75% and split Wall Street 2022 outlooks provide macro context but not a wave-stage classification.

AGG.rationale: AGG returned -1.8% in 2021 with yield far below 6.2% CPI and rising Fed taper signals pushing prices lower — bond prices are declining from prior highs in a rising-rate environment, matching the post-crest hangover pattern.

BIL.rationale: BIL is a cash-equivalent T-bill vehicle; with Fed funds at 0-0.25% in 2021, it delivered near-zero nominal return and carries no wave signal.

IAU.rationale: Gold declined in 2021 despite surging inflation, reflecting real-yield dynamics rather than a technology-wave signal; IAU carries no wave-bucket assignment and the single bullet is insufficient to call a directional stage.

VIG.rationale: VIG is a general-markets dividend-growth ETF; its +23.76% 2021 return reflects broad equity conditions rather than any specific technology wave, and no wave-bucket bullets were surfaced.

self_critique_downgrades.reason: Retained: the -1.8% AGG return in 2021 and the inflation/taper context are general knowledge clearly available before 2021-12-31 and confirmed by search results. One sourced bullet (Seeking Alpha article on AGG low-risk-low-return) was published before 2022-01-01. The 24/7 Wall St. source describing 'two consecutive down years' is a post-date article and was used only to confirm the 2021 loss direction, not as primary evidence — primary evidence is the -1.8% return figure and the October 2021 6.2% CPI reading. No downgrade warranted.

Retained: Q3 2021 SEC filing (Oct 26, 2021) is the primary evidence for revenue +41% and Cloud +45%. The 65% full-year stock return figure came from a CNBC/Yahoo Finance article referencing the Q3 earnings; while the exact article date could not be confirmed as pre-2022, the underlying Q3 earnings filing date of Oct 26, 2021 is definitively pre-cutoff. Stage grounded in that primary bullet. No downgrade warranted.

Retained: The Motley Fool article dated Dec 15, 2021 ('Will Apple Hit a $3 Trillion Market Cap in 2022?') is confirmed pre-2022-01-01. Q4 FY21 earnings (Oct 28, 2021) are confirmed pre-cutoff. The 34% stock return figure for calendar-year 2021 came from a search result summary and while no exact article date was confirmed, it is corroborated by the approaching-$3T-cap narrative from Dec 15, 2021. No forbidden phrases used. No downgrade warranted.
